restrictions.description: Johnson County (Greenwood/Franklin) south of Indianapolis benefits from Indiana's data center tax exemption (IC 6-2.5-5-37 for qualifying computer equipment) and Duke Energy Indiana's competitive rates. The county's I-65 corridor provides fiber connectivity south to Louisville and north to Indianapolis carrier hotels. Franklin College's computer science programs provide local workforce support; the county's rapid commercial growth reflects spillover from Indianapolis's data center market.
restrictions.notes: Indiana IC 6-2.5-5-37 exemption covers qualifying computer equipment, cooling systems, and related infrastructure with minimum investment of $10M and 20 jobs. Johnson County's industrial parks on I-65 have attracted Amazon and logistics operators that co-locate with data infrastructure. Duke Energy Indiana's rates are competitive in the MISO Midwest market.

sources.label: Indiana Code IC 6-2.5-5-37 Data Center Sales Tax Exemption

restrictions.name: Hancock County
restrictions.title: Indiana Data Center Tax Exemption — Hancock County I-70 East Indianapolis
restrictions.description: Hancock County (Greenfield) on I-70 east of Indianapolis benefits from Indiana's §IC 6-2.5-5-37 data center equipment tax exemption and proximity to the Indianapolis Airport's Mount Comfort satellite general aviation airport. Duke Energy Indiana serves the county; the I-70 corridor provides fiber connectivity to Columbus OH and Indianapolis. The county's participation in the Indianapolis Regional Development Commission provides coordinated permitting for large technology investments.
restrictions.notes: Hancock County's eastern Indianapolis position provides access to Indianapolis's metropolitan fiber and carrier hotels while offering lower real estate costs. Indiana's economic development incentives (IEDC) include Skills Enhancement Fund grants for data center workforce training. Duke Energy Indiana's grid connects to AEP Ohio at the Indiana-Ohio border, enabling network redundancy.

sources.label: Indiana Code IC 6-2.5-5-37 Data Center Sales Tax Exemption

restrictions.name: Morgan County
restrictions.title: Indiana Data Center Tax Exemption — Morgan County Indianapolis Southwest
restrictions.description: Morgan County (Martinsville) southwest of Indianapolis participates in Indiana's data center tax exemption program (IC 6-2.5-5-37). Duke Energy Indiana serves the county; the SR-37/I-69 corridor provides fiber access to Bloomington and Indianapolis. The county's Indiana University Health Morgan Hospital campus generates local healthcare data management demand. IEDC incentives for technology investment apply to qualifying data center projects meeting Indiana's job creation thresholds.
restrictions.notes: Morgan County's US-40 and SR-37 corridors connect to I-70 (east-west) and I-69 (north-south), providing fiber path diversity. Indiana's data center exemption requires 20+ qualifying jobs and $10M+ investment. Duke Energy Indiana's rates are among the more competitive in the Midwest.

sources.label: Indiana Code IC 6-2.5-5-37 Data Center Sales Tax Exemption

restrictions.name: Shelby County
restrictions.title: Indiana Data Center Tax Exemption — Shelby County Shelbyville
restrictions.description: Shelby County (Shelbyville) east of Indianapolis qualifies for Indiana's data center equipment tax exemption under IC 6-2.5-5-37. Duke Energy Indiana provides grid access; the I-74 corridor connects to Cincinnati (east) and Indianapolis (west) with fiber infrastructure. Shelbyville's manufacturing base (Toyota, GE) has built industrial-grade electrical infrastructure adaptable for data center use. Flat land and modest land costs relative to Indianapolis make the county attractive for large-footprint data center development.
restrictions.notes: Shelby County's I-74 position puts it equidistant between Indianapolis and Cincinnati metro fiber hubs, enabling network path diversity. Toyota's Shelbyville paint facility established high-reliability industrial electrical feeds in the county that support data center power planning. IEDC incentives available for qualifying job creation.

sources.label: Indiana Code IC 6-2.5-5-37 Data Center Sales Tax Exemption

restrictions.name: Bartow County
restrictions.title: Data Center Permitting Requirements — Cartersville Northwest Atlanta Corridor
restrictions.description: Bartow County (Cartersville) lies in the northwest Atlanta data center expansion corridor along I-75. The county planning commission reviews data center applications under its special-use permit process, with conditions addressing noise mitigation, stormwater management, and visual compatibility with adjacent land uses. Multiple operators have evaluated sites in the I-75 / GA-20 area. Georgia's sales and use tax exemption on qualifying data center equipment (O.C.G.A. §48-8-3(82)) applies statewide, providing an incentive baseline.
restrictions.notes: Special-use permit review required for large-scale data center facilities. Level set to 1 reflecting light but real permitting process.

sources.label: Georgia Department of Revenue — Data Center Tax Exemptions

restrictions.name: Carroll County
restrictions.title: Data Center Zoning Restrictions — Carroll County Special-Use Permit Process
restrictions.description: Carroll County (Carrollton) has enacted zoning text amendments requiring special-use permits for large-scale data center developments, with detailed conditions on noise, landscaping, setbacks, and compatibility with surrounding uses. The county board has expressed concern about the limited local employment generated by data centers relative to the acreage and infrastructure consumed. Several data center proposals have faced significant community opposition. Carroll County is on the western edge of Atlanta's expanding data center market.
restrictions.notes: Special-use permit required. Level 2 reflects meaningful public process with documented board skepticism beyond a routine permit review.

sources.label: Georgia Department of Revenue — Data Center Tax Exemptions

restrictions.name: Fayette County
restrictions.title: Conditional Use Permit Requirements — Fayette County South Atlanta
restrictions.description: Fayette County (Fayetteville) is a residential suburban county south of Atlanta with a strong local preference for preserving community character. The county requires conditional use permits for data center development, with emphasis on noise attenuation from cooling systems, visual screening, and compatibility with adjacent residential zones. While not a primary data center market, operators seeking alternatives to higher-cost Fulton and DeKalb counties have evaluated Fayette sites.
restrictions.notes: Conditional use permit process in place. No enacted moratorium. Level 1 reflects real but moderate regulatory requirement.

sources.label: Georgia Department of Revenue — Data Center Tax Exemptions

restrictions.name: Paulding County
restrictions.title: Proposed Data Center Siting Regulations — Paulding County Northwest Atlanta
restrictions.description: Paulding County (Dallas, GA) is a rapidly growing northwest Atlanta suburban county that has received data center development proposals as operators seek alternatives to counties with existing moratoriums. The county board is studying enhanced special-use permit requirements addressing noise, energy use, employment impacts, and community character. Public hearings have drawn significant resident opposition. Paulding County is adjacent to Cherokee, Cobb, and Carroll counties, all of which have faced similar development pressures.
restrictions.notes: Enhanced regulations under study. Existing CUP process in place. Level 2 reflects documented board discussion and community opposition beyond routine review.

restrictions.name: Spalding County
restrictions.title: Spalding County Georgia Data Center Investment — Griffin Market
restrictions.description: Spalding County (Griffin) benefits from Georgia's sales and use tax exemption on qualifying data center equipment (O.C.G.A. §48-8-3(82)) and Georgia Power's competitive industrial rates. Griffin is approximately 40 miles south of Atlanta along the I-75/US-19 corridor. The county has not enacted data center-specific restrictions and actively competes for technology investment through the Development Authority of Spalding County.
restrictions.notes: Pro data center environment. No restrictions enacted.

sources.label: Georgia Department of Revenue — Data Center Tax Exemptions

restrictions.name: Burke County
restrictions.title: Burke County Western NC Data Center Siting Standards — Morganton
restrictions.description: Burke County (Morganton) has proposed enhanced data center siting standards in response to development interest from operators attracted to western North Carolina's power infrastructure and land costs. The county planning commission is reviewing setback, noise, and visual screening requirements for large computing facilities. Burke County is adjacent to Caldwell County (Lenoir) and benefits from Duke Energy's western NC transmission system.
restrictions.notes: Proposed siting standards under review. No enacted moratorium. Level 1 reflects light to moderate proposed regulatory effort.

restrictions.name: Caldwell County
restrictions.title: Caldwell County — Western NC Technology Corridor Adjacent to Apple Maiden Campus
restrictions.description: Caldwell County (Lenoir) is adjacent to Catawba County (Maiden), where Apple operates one of its major US data center campuses. Caldwell County benefits from Duke Energy's western NC transmission infrastructure, North Carolina's data center investment incentives, and the spillover development activity from Apple's Catawba campus. The county has not enacted data center-specific restrictions and participates in the Catawba Valley region's active data center market.
restrictions.notes: Pro data center environment. No restrictions.

sources.label: North Carolina Department of Commerce — Data Center Incentives

restrictions.name: Morehouse Parish
restrictions.title: Morehouse Parish — Adjacent to Meta Richland Parish Data Center Corridor
restrictions.description: Morehouse Parish (Bastrop) borders Richland Parish, where Meta Platforms announced an $800M+ data center campus in 2023. Morehouse Parish benefits from Louisiana's Industrial Tax Exemption Program (ITEP), which can provide up to 100% property tax exemption for 10 years for qualifying industrial investments. Entergy Louisiana serves the area. The parish has no data center-specific restrictions and welcomes technology investment through the Morehouse Economic Development Authority.
restrictions.notes: Pro investment environment adjacent to major Meta campus county.

restrictions.name: Rapides Parish
restrictions.title: Rapides Parish — Central Louisiana Data Center Hub (Alexandria)
restrictions.description: Rapides Parish (Alexandria) is central Louisiana's commercial and governmental hub. The parish benefits from Louisiana's ITEP 100% property tax exemption for qualifying industrial facilities, Cleco Power's competitive industrial rates, and I-49 access between Baton Rouge and Shreveport. Cleco (now Liberty Utilities) has marketed central Louisiana's available generation capacity to technology-intensive operators. The Louisiana Data Center Incentive (R.S. 47:6033) provides a 15% refundable income tax credit for qualifying new data centers.
restrictions.notes: Louisiana R.S. 47:6033 data center tax credit applies. ITEP exemption available. No restrictions enacted.

sources.label: Louisiana Economic Development — Data Center Incentives

restrictions.name: St. John the Baptist Parish
restrictions.title: St. John the Baptist Parish — Mississippi River Industrial Corridor
restrictions.description: St. John the Baptist Parish (LaPlace) lies in the Greater New Orleans industrial corridor along the Mississippi River. The parish hosts major petrochemical, logistics, and industrial facilities with significant power infrastructure. Louisiana's ITEP exemption and industrial property tax structure support data center and high-density computing investment. Entergy Louisiana provides power with direct access to the Louisiana transmission grid. The parish is approximately 30 miles west of New Orleans.
restrictions.notes: River Parish industrial corridor. ITEP available. No data center-specific restrictions.

sources.label: St. John the Baptist Parish Economic Development

restrictions.name: Baltimore County
restrictions.title: Baltimore County Data Center Market — BWI Corridor
restrictions.description: Baltimore County hosts a significant data center cluster in the Baltimore-Washington Corridor, leveraging proximity to federal agencies and a dense fiber network. The county's Opportunity Zone and Enterprise Zone programs support technology investment. Baltimore County provides property tax credits for data center equipment under Maryland's data center tax credit provisions. Major colocation providers operate facilities near BWI Airport. No data center-specific restrictions have been enacted.
restrictions.notes: Pro investment environment. Maryland property tax credit applies.

sources.label: Maryland Department of Assessments and Taxation — Data Center Credit

restrictions.name: Baltimore city
restrictions.title: Baltimore City Data Center and Network Hub — Inner Harbor Connectivity
restrictions.description: Baltimore city hosts carrier-neutral data centers and network exchange points serving the Mid-Atlantic. The city's proximity to Washington DC (40 miles) and diverse fiber infrastructure make it a natural disaster-recovery and interconnection hub. Maryland's data center property tax credit applies. BGE (Exelon) provides reliable power. No data center-specific restrictions exist; the city actively courts technology investment under the Baltimore Development Corporation's strategic plan.
restrictions.notes: Urban carrier-neutral hub. No restrictions.

sources.label: Maryland Department of Assessments and Taxation — Data Center Credit

restrictions.name: Wayne County
restrictions.title: Wayne County — Detroit Metro Data Center and AI Regulation Environment
restrictions.description: Wayne County (Detroit) is Michigan's most populous county and the anchor of the Detroit metro technology market. Wayne County hosts enterprise and government data centers tied to the automotive and financial services industries. Michigan has proposed data center legislation and data privacy bills in 2024-2025 sessions, but no statewide data center tax exemption has been enacted. Detroit's data center market faces grid reliability concerns following DTE Energy capacity issues and some municipal AI oversight discussions. The Detroit City Council has considered AI surveillance and automated-decision policies.
restrictions.notes: Level 1 reflects pending Michigan legislation and local AI policy discussions. No enacted moratorium.

sources.label: Michigan Legislature — Technology and Data Privacy Bills

restrictions.name: Platte County
restrictions.title: Platte County Nebraska — Columbus Data Center Hub (LB 1043 Incentive)
restrictions.description: Platte County (Columbus, NE) is a growing data center market in central Nebraska, benefiting from the state's LB 1043 data center investment incentive program and competitive power rates from NPPD (Nebraska Public Power District). Columbus is strategically located on I-80 and the Union Pacific rail corridor with diverse fiber connectivity. The county actively recruits technology investment through the Columbus Area Economic Development. Nebraska's agricultural and manufacturing base provides large, stable electrical loads compatible with data center power infrastructure.
restrictions.notes: LB 1043 incentive applies. NPPD power at competitive rates. No restrictions.

sources.label: Nebraska Legislature — LB 1043 Data Center Incentive

restrictions.name: Gilliam County
restrictions.title: Gilliam County — Columbia River Wind Energy and Data Center Development
restrictions.description: Gilliam County lies in the Columbia River Gorge with among the highest-quality wind energy resources in North America. The county is served by the BPA (Bonneville Power Administration) transmission system. Proximity to The Dalles in neighboring Wasco County — home to Google's largest North American data center campus — has made Gilliam County a target for operators seeking renewable energy with transmission access. Oregon's property tax exemption for qualifying data centers applies. No data center-specific restrictions have been enacted.
restrictions.notes: Wind energy + BPA transmission corridor. Oregon property tax exemption applies. No restrictions.

restrictions.name: Sherman County
restrictions.title: Sherman County — Columbia Gorge Energy Corridor Adjacent to The Dalles
restrictions.description: Sherman County (Moro) lies between Wasco County (The Dalles, home to Google's data center campus) and Gilliam County along the Columbia River. The county shares the same BPA transmission infrastructure and Columbia River hydroelectric access that makes the gorge corridor a premier data center destination. Oregon's property tax exemption for qualifying data centers applies. Sherman County has not enacted data center-specific restrictions and participates in the regional data center development environment.
restrictions.notes: Columbia Gorge corridor. Oregon property tax exemption applies. No restrictions.

restrictions.name: Horry County
restrictions.title: Horry County South Carolina — Myrtle Beach Technology and Data Center Environment
restrictions.description: Horry County (Myrtle Beach) benefits from South Carolina's data center sales tax exemption on equipment and electricity for qualifying capital investments. The county's coastal location, growing technology workforce, and Santee Cooper power infrastructure support data center development. HTC (Horry Telephone Cooperative) provides fiber connectivity throughout the county. No data center-specific restrictions have been enacted. The county attracts enterprise and government data centers serving the Southeastern coastal market.
restrictions.notes: SC sales tax exemption applies. No restrictions.

sources.label: South Carolina DOR — Manufacturing & Data Center Exemptions

restrictions.name: Fentress County
restrictions.title: Fentress County — Upper Cumberland TVA Territory Energy-Intensive Computing Discussions
restrictions.description: Fentress County (Jamestown) is in TVA's Upper Cumberland service area, a region that has faced grid capacity discussions as energy-intensive computing operators evaluate rural Tennessee sites. Community discussions have addressed the compatibility of large electrical loads with existing residential and agricultural uses, the visual and noise impacts of data center cooling systems, and employment benefits relative to acreage consumed. No moratorium or formal ordinance has been enacted. The county is in a rural character preservation area, and any large commercial or industrial development requires county commission approval.
restrictions.notes: No enacted ordinance. Level 1 reflects formal county-commission review process and documented local discussions about grid impacts.

restrictions.name: Rhea County
restrictions.title: Rhea County — Watts Bar Nuclear TVA Area — Energy-Intensive Computing Review
restrictions.description: Rhea County (Dayton) is home to TVA's Watts Bar Nuclear Plant (Unit 1: 1996, Unit 2: 2016 — the most recently completed nuclear plant in the US). The county has attracted interest from energy-intensive computing operators seeking to co-locate with nuclear generation. The county commission applies routine special-use review to large industrial proposals including data centers. Community discussions have addressed compatibility with the county's agricultural and recreational character (Rhea County borders Watts Bar Lake). No moratorium or formal data center restriction has been enacted.
restrictions.notes: No enacted moratorium. Level 1 reflects special-use review requirement and documented local discussion about industrial land use near the nuclear plant.

sources.label: Tennessee Valley Authority — Watts Bar Nuclear Plant

restrictions.name: Roane County
restrictions.title: Roane County — Oak Ridge National Lab and TVA Energy Complex
restrictions.description: Roane County (Kingston) is adjacent to Anderson County (Oak Ridge) and borders the Oak Ridge National Laboratory complex — one of the world's premier scientific computing facilities. TVA's Kingston Fossil Plant (being converted to a gas peaker) and proximity to ORNL's high-performance computing infrastructure make Roane County an attractive location for research computing and AI training workloads. Tennessee's sales tax exemption on qualifying data center equipment (Tenn. Code Ann. §67-6-206) applies statewide. No data center-specific restrictions exist; the county actively supports research and technology investment.
restrictions.notes: Pro investment environment adjacent to ORNL. TN sales tax exemption applies.

sources.label: Oak Ridge National Laboratory — Computing and Data Sciences
Tennessee Department of Revenue — Data Center Exemption

restrictions.name: Kenai Peninsula Borough
restrictions.title: Kenai Peninsula Borough — Cook Inlet Oil and Gas Industry Computing Hub
restrictions.description: The Kenai Peninsula Borough hosts Cook Inlet oil and gas operations with significant industrial IT and edge computing infrastructure. Homer Electric Association (HEA) and Chugach Electric provide power to the borough. The natural gas processing facilities at Kenai LNG and associated oil fields generate data management workloads. No data center-specific regulations or restrictions exist. Alaska has no state sales tax and no state income tax, making equipment procurement favorable. The remote geography and limited fiber backhaul limit commercial colocation development, but the borough hosts enterprise and government computing assets.
restrictions.notes: Industrial edge computing environment. No restrictions. No commercial data center incentive program.

restrictions.name: Clarke County
restrictions.title: Clarke County — Rural Virginia Data Center Siting Restrictions Near Loudoun Corridor
restrictions.description: Clarke County (Berryville) is located adjacent to Loudoun and Frederick counties in the Northern Virginia data center expansion corridor, approximately 65 miles west of Washington DC. As data center development pressure has moved outward from Loudoun and Prince William counties, Clarke County has enacted special-use permit requirements for large-scale computing facilities with emphasis on preserving the county's rural agricultural character and scenic viewsheds. The county has a comprehensive plan emphasizing conservation, and the board of supervisors has expressed skepticism about energy-intensive development incompatible with the county's land-use vision.
restrictions.notes: Special-use permit required with strong rural preservation emphasis. Level 2 reflects documented county resistance to data center development beyond routine permitting.

sources.label: Virginia DHCD — Data Center Incentive Package

restrictions.name: Frederick County
restrictions.title: Frederick County — Winchester Virginia Data Center Development
restrictions.description: Frederick County (Winchester area) is in the northern Shenandoah Valley along I-81, approximately 75 miles west of Washington DC. The county has received data center development proposals as the Northern Virginia market expands westward along major fiber routes. Frederick County requires special-use permits for large data center facilities with conditions including noise mitigation and visual screening. The county is more receptive to development than rural neighbors Rappahannock and Page, though it balances agricultural preservation goals against technology investment.
restrictions.notes: Special-use permit required. Moderate regulatory environment. Level 1 reflects light to moderate permitting process.

sources.label: Virginia DHCD — Data Center Incentive Package

restrictions.name: Orange County
restrictions.title: Orange County — Piedmont Virginia Data Center Permitting
restrictions.description: Orange County (Orange, VA) lies in the Piedmont Virginia region south of Culpeper County, which already has significant data center activity. The county has received data center development proposals and applies special-use permit review to large computing facilities. Orange County balances data center investment opportunities against preservation of the county's historic character (Montpelier, Madison's home, is located here) and rural agricultural landscape. Virginia's data center incentive package applies statewide.
restrictions.notes: Special-use permit required. Historic and agricultural character preservation concern. Level 1 reflects moderate regulatory environment.

sources.label: Virginia DHCD — Data Center Incentive Package

restrictions.name: Warren County
restrictions.title: Warren County — Front Royal Data Center Expansion Pressure and Siting Restrictions
restrictions.description: Warren County (Front Royal) is located at the northern end of the Shenandoah Valley, adjacent to Fauquier County (which has enacted level-3 restrictions) and approximately 70 miles west of Washington DC. Data center operators have evaluated Warren County sites as the Northern Virginia market becomes increasingly constrained. The board of supervisors has discussed enhanced data center siting requirements, citing concerns about grid capacity (Warren Electric Cooperative), rural character, and the limited employment generated relative to infrastructure demands. The county has enacted or is enacting special-use permit standards with meaningful conditions.
restrictions.notes: Enhanced special-use permit review in place or being established. Level 2 reflects active board concern and documented public discussion.

sources.label: Virginia DHCD — Data Center Incentive Package

restrictions.name: Hanover County
restrictions.title: Richmond Northern Suburb — Growing Data Center Market
restrictions.description: Hanover County occupies the northern suburban ring of the Richmond MSA. Proximity to I-95 and Lower Westham fiber nodes, combined with Virginia Economic Development Partnership incentives, has attracted colocation and edge-compute investment as land and power costs in Northern Virginia have escalated.

restrictions.name: Nassau County
restrictions.title: Jacksonville Northern Suburb — Industrial Corridor
restrictions.description: Nassau County anchors the northern Jacksonville industrial corridor. The county's proximity to I-95 and Jacksonville's major fiber exchange, combined with Florida's absence of personal income tax and county-level industrial incentives, makes it an emerging location for edge data centers and disaster-recovery sites.

restrictions.name: Manatee County
restrictions.title: Tampa Bay South — County EDC Tech Recruitment
restrictions.description: Manatee County (Bradenton) anchors the southern Tampa Bay metro and has actively recruited technology infrastructure through the Manatee County Economic Development Council's incentive programs. The county benefits from lower industrial land costs versus Hillsborough, Florida's tax environment, and diverse power infrastructure.

restrictions.name: Hernando County
restrictions.title: Tampa Metro North — Emerging Tech Industrial Zone
restrictions.description: Hernando County (Brooksville) sits on the northern fringe of the Tampa metro and is emerging as a tech-industrial zone following extension of Duke Energy Florida grid infrastructure. County Enterprise Zone designations and lower land costs position it as a candidate for remote-site data centers.

restrictions.name: Alachua County
restrictions.title: Gainesville — University of Florida AI and Cloud Research Hub
restrictions.description: Alachua County (Gainesville) hosts the University of Florida, which has built one of the fastest AI supercomputers in higher education (HiPerGator). The county benefits from this research infrastructure, fiber redundancy, and Florida's favorable business climate. UF's partnerships with NVIDIA and commercial cloud operators drive demand for ancillary compute infrastructure.

restrictions.name: Guadalupe County
restrictions.title: San Antonio Northeast Corridor — Seguin Industrial Growth
restrictions.description: Guadalupe County (Seguin) lies northeast of Bexar County and represents the primary expansion corridor for San Antonio's growing technology infrastructure market. Lower land and power costs versus Bexar, TX DOT connectivity along US 90 and I-10, and Guadalupe Valley Electric Cooperative service areas make this a target for data center site selection.

restrictions.name: Bastrop County
restrictions.title: Austin Eastern Corridor — SpaceX-Driven Infrastructure Investment
restrictions.description: Bastrop County anchors the eastern Austin metro and has experienced rapid infrastructure investment driven by SpaceX's Starbase-adjacent Texas operations, Tesla's Giga Texas footprint in adjacent Travis County, and overflow from the Austin tech market. LCRA power access, fiber along SH 71, and low industrial land costs attract data center and edge-compute operators.

sources.label: Lower Colorado River Authority — Economic Development

restrictions.name: Lake County
restrictions.title: Chicago North Suburb — Major Fiber and Data Center Cluster
restrictions.description: Lake County is home to Waukegan and Gurnee, anchoring Chicago's northern data center corridor. ComEd Tier A reliability, proximity to the Chicago metro fiber ring, and county Enterprise Zone programs have attracted major colocation operators. The county's industrial base provides existing heavy-power infrastructure.

sources.label: Illinois Department of Commerce — Enterprise Zones

restrictions.name: Kendall County
restrictions.title: Chicago Exurb — I-80 Corridor Industrial Growth
restrictions.description: Kendall County (Yorkville) sits at the southwestern edge of the Chicago MSA along the I-80 corridor and is experiencing rapid industrial growth. Lower land costs versus DuPage and Cook counties, ComEd power access, and proximity to Chicago fiber exchange routes make it a candidate for large-footprint data center campuses.

restrictions.name: Franklin County
restrictions.title: Raleigh Northeast — Research Triangle Overflow Market
restrictions.description: Franklin County (Louisburg) lies northeast of Wake County and serves as a Research Triangle overflow market as land and power costs in the triangle core have escalated. Duke Energy Progress service territory, county incentive programs under the NC Economic Development Tier 2 designation, and I-540 corridor extension are driving site-selection interest.

sources.label: NC Department of Commerce — Tier Designations

restrictions.name: Gaston County
restrictions.title: Charlotte West — Duke Energy Incentives and Industrial Land
restrictions.description: Gaston County (Gastonia) is the primary western expansion corridor for the Charlotte data center market. Duke Energy Carolinas service territory with competitive industrial power rates, substantial Tier 2 county incentives under NC economic development programs, and the County's industrial heritage (textile/manufacturing) providing heavy-power infrastructure make it attractive for large-scale data center investment.

sources.label: Duke Energy — Data Center Site Selection

restrictions.name: Washington County
restrictions.title: Minneapolis Eastern Suburb — Woodbury and Cottage Grove Data Centers
restrictions.description: Washington County (Woodbury, Cottage Grove, Stillwater) anchors the eastern Minneapolis metro and hosts several colocation and enterprise data center facilities. Xcel Energy service with competitive commercial rates, proximity to Twin Cities metro fiber rings, and county economic development programs make this a stable data center environment.

restrictions.name: Clay County
restrictions.title: Kansas City North Suburb — Liberty and North KC Data Center Corridor
restrictions.description: Clay County (Liberty, North Kansas City) lies north of Kansas City proper and hosts a growing data center corridor along I-35 and MO-291. KCP&L/Evergy service territory, industrial land availability at lower costs than Johnson County KS, and Missouri Chapter 100 industrial development bond incentives make it a target for data center and hyperscale investment.

restrictions.name: Perry County
restrictions.title: Columbus Southeast — AEP Ohio Low-Rate Power Territory
restrictions.description: Perry County lies in the American Electric Power Ohio service territory southeast of Columbus. AEP Ohio's industrial power rates have historically been among the lowest in the state. The county's rural character, available land, and the broader Ohio data center expansion driven by Intel and hyperscale investment in adjacent counties make it a candidate for remote-site compute deployments.

sources.label: Ohio Development Services Agency — Site Selection

restrictions.name: Hocking County
restrictions.title: Rural Ohio — AEP Territory Low-Cost Industrial Power
restrictions.description: Hocking County sits south of Columbus in AEP Ohio territory. Historically low industrial power rates, rural land availability, and adjacency to the Columbus data center market's overflow demand are the primary data center considerations. The county has no specific data center regulations.

restrictions.name: Elbert County
restrictions.title: Denver Southeastern Exurb — IREA Territory Industrial Growth
restrictions.description: Elbert County (Elizabeth) sits at the southeastern edge of the Denver MSA in the Intermountain Rural Electric Association service territory. IREA offers some of the most competitive rural power rates in Colorado. The county's rural character, low land costs, and adjacency to the growing Denver-Castle Rock-Parker tech corridor position it for data center consideration.

restrictions.name: Yavapai County
restrictions.title: Prescott — Cooler Climate and State Enterprise Zone Designation
restrictions.description: Yavapai County (Prescott, Prescott Valley) offers a meaningful temperature advantage over Phoenix — 10°–15°F cooler — reducing cooling costs for data center operations. The county benefits from Arizona's state-level data center property and transaction privilege tax exemption (ARS Title 42), Arizona Public Service power, and Enterprise Zone incentives for tech and industrial investment.

restrictions.name: Cheatham County
restrictions.title: Nashville Western Corridor — TVA Territory Industrial Land
restrictions.description: Cheatham County (Ashland City) lies west of Nashville in TVA service territory. Tennessee Valley Authority power rates have historically been competitive for industrial users. The county's rural-industrial character provides large parcel availability adjacent to the Nashville metro data center market, with I-40 and US 70 connectivity.

sources.label: Tennessee Department of Economic and Community Development

restrictions.name: Summit County
restrictions.title: Park City — Rocky Mountain Power and Growing Tech Presence
restrictions.description: Summit County (Park City, Coalville) has seen growing tech infrastructure interest as part of Utah's broader tech sector expansion. Rocky Mountain Power (PacifiCorp) service with competitive renewable energy options, Utah's favorable data center sales tax exemption on equipment and power, and proximity to Salt Lake City make the county relevant for edge and regional deployments.

sources.label: Utah Governor's Office of Economic Opportunity — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Codington County
restrictions.title: Watertown — East-West Fiber Corridor Hub
restrictions.description: Codington County (Watertown) sits at a key fiber corridor intersection in eastern South Dakota. South Dakota's no-corporate-income-tax and no-personal-income-tax environment, combined with low industrial power rates from NorthWestern Energy and Codington-Clark Electric Cooperative, make it attractive for data center developers seeking rural build-to-suit sites.

sources.label: South Dakota Governor's Office of Economic Development

restrictions.name: Brown County
restrictions.title: Aberdeen — State Tax Advantage and Fiber Access
restrictions.description: Brown County (Aberdeen) benefits from South Dakota's zero-income-tax regime and is served by Northwestern Energy with competitive commercial rates. Aberdeen's position as a regional hub in northeastern South Dakota with fiber access to Minneapolis and Sioux Falls makes it a candidate for distributed data center deployments.

sources.label: South Dakota Governor's Office of Economic Development

restrictions.name: Torrance County
restrictions.title: Estancia Valley — State Incentive Zones East of Albuquerque
restrictions.description: Torrance County (Estancia, Moriarty) lies east of Bernalillo County along I-40 and benefits from New Mexico's data center tax incentive program (50% deduction on gross receipts for qualified facilities). The Estancia Valley offers low-cost land and proximity to Albuquerque fiber while avoiding the metro's increasing data center competition.

sources.label: New Mexico Economic Development Department — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Otero County
restrictions.title: Alamogordo — Defense Data Center Interest and Solar Energy
restrictions.description: Otero County (Alamogordo) hosts Holloman Air Force Base and has seen defense-adjacent data center interest. The county benefits from New Mexico's data center gross receipts deduction program, growing solar power generation, and low land costs. White Sands Missile Range adjacency creates both opportunities and security-zone constraints for some site typologies.

sources.label: New Mexico Economic Development Department — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Nye County
restrictions.title: Pahrump — Land Availability and Nevada Tax Advantages
restrictions.description: Nye County (Pahrump) sits 60 miles west of Las Vegas in Nevada's most expansive county. Nevada's absence of corporate income tax and personal income tax, combined with NV Energy and Wells Rural Electric Company competitive rates and abundant low-cost desert land, make Pahrump an emerging data center consideration for operators priced out of Clark County. Some crypto mining activity already established.

sources.label: Nevada Governor's Office of Economic Development — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Walker County
restrictions.title: Chattanooga Metro Spillover — TVA Industrial Territory
restrictions.description: Walker County (Rock Spring, LaFayette) is part of the Chattanooga, TN–GA MSA and served by TVA power through Catoosa Electric Membership Corporation. TVA's competitive industrial rates have historically driven data center consideration in this region. No county-level data center restrictions. State of Georgia data center tax exemption (O.C.G.A. § 48-8-3.2) applies.

sources.label: Georgia Department of Economic Development — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Murray County
restrictions.title: Northwest Georgia — TVA Industrial Corridor
restrictions.description: Murray County (Chatsworth) sits in the northwestern Georgia industrial corridor served by TVA power through Cobb Electric Membership Corporation. The county's position along US 76 and proximity to I-75 provides logistics access. Georgia's O.C.G.A. § 48-8-3.2 data center equipment sales tax exemption applies statewide.

restrictions.name: Catoosa County
restrictions.title: Chattanooga Georgia Suburb — I-75 Industrial Hub
restrictions.description: Catoosa County (Ringgold, Fort Oglethorpe) is the Georgia suburban anchor of the Chattanooga MSA, straddling I-75. TVA power access, the Georgia data center equipment sales tax exemption, and low industrial land costs relative to Tennessee-side Hamilton County make it a growing candidate for edge and colocation data center development.

restrictions.name: Lee County
restrictions.title: Auburn — Auburn University Tech and AI Research Corridor
restrictions.description: Lee County (Auburn, Opelika) benefits from Auburn University's engineering and AI research programs, Alabama Power industrial rates competitive within the Southeast, and state-level incentives under Alabama's Growing Alabama Credit program. The county's I-85 corridor and proximity to Atlanta and Columbus GA position it as a secondary data center market.

restrictions.name: Chester County
restrictions.title: Philadelphia Western Suburb — Pharma and Data Center Corridor
restrictions.description: Chester County (West Chester, Exton, Malvern) is the wealthiest county in Pennsylvania and hosts a dense corporate campus corridor along US 30 and PA 202. Major pharmaceutical, financial services, and healthcare IT operations in the county create substantial enterprise data center demand. PECO Energy service territory with high reliability.

restrictions.name: Boyd County
restrictions.title: Ashland — Industrial Power Infrastructure on Ohio River
restrictions.description: Boyd County (Ashland) sits on the Ohio River tri-state corner of KY–WV–OH and benefits from Kentucky's legacy industrial power infrastructure. AEP Kentucky (Kentucky Power) industrial rates and state data center incentives under Kentucky's KY LLET and Sales Tax exemption make it competitive. The county's industrial heritage provides existing high-voltage infrastructure.

sources.label: Kentucky Cabinet for Economic Development — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Franklin County
restrictions.title: St. Albans — Cross-Border Data Corridor and Green Power
restrictions.description: Franklin County (St. Albans) sits on the Vermont–Quebec border and benefits from Vermont's very high percentage of renewable power (primarily Hydro-Québec imports and in-state hydro). The county's position along the Burlington metro's northern expansion zone and Vermont's no-sales-tax environment for certain data equipment provide modest attractors.

restrictions.name: Juneau City and Borough
restrictions.title: Juneau — State Capital Government Data Infrastructure
restrictions.description: Juneau (Alaska's capital) hosts state government data infrastructure and benefits from Alaska Power & Telephone service plus hydro-dominated power generation from the Snettisham system. Government IT demands and the state's absence of corporate income tax or sales tax are relevant factors. Physical isolation (no road to Juneau) limits large commercial builds but supports government/enterprise deployments.

sources.label: City and Borough of Juneau — Economic Development

restrictions.name: Jefferson County
restrictions.title: Eastern Panhandle — Northern Virginia Data Center Spillover
restrictions.description: Jefferson County (Charles Town, Shepherdstown, Harpers Ferry) anchors West Virginia's Eastern Panhandle, a commuter zone for the Washington DC metro. As Northern Virginia Loudoun County data center costs have escalated, operators have explored Jefferson County as a cost-effective alternative — lower land costs, Appalachian Power (AEP) service, and proximity to I-81 fiber corridors. West Virginia exempts data center equipment from sales tax (WV Code § 11-15B-14).

sources.label: West Virginia Development Office — Business Incentives

restrictions.name: Johnson County
restrictions.title: Southwest DFW — Cleburne Industrial Corridor
restrictions.description: Johnson County (Cleburne) anchors the southwestern DFW exurban corridor along US 67. Lower industrial land costs relative to Tarrant County, Oncor Energy service with competitive commercial rates, and the broader Texas economic climate with no corporate income tax make Johnson County an emerging data center consideration as the DFW market continues to expand outward.

restrictions.name: Rockwall County
restrictions.title: East Dallas Suburb — Growing Industrial and Tech Zone
restrictions.description: Rockwall County (Rockwall) is one of the fastest-growing counties in the Dallas-Fort Worth MSA, situated on the eastern shore of Lake Ray Hubbard along I-30. Oncor service territory, lower commercial land costs than Dallas County, and the county's tech-friendly economic development environment make it attractive for edge data center and enterprise deployments.

restrictions.name: Comal County
restrictions.title: San Antonio North Corridor — New Braunfels Industrial Growth
restrictions.description: Comal County (New Braunfels, San Marcos area) anchors the northern San Antonio expansion corridor along I-35. One of the fastest-growing counties in Texas, benefiting from GVTC Communications fiber, Guadalupe Valley Electric service, Texas no-income-tax advantage, and spillover from both Austin and San Antonio metro industrial demand. No county-level data center restrictions.

restrictions.name: Rensselaer County
restrictions.title: Troy and Tech Valley — RPI and Capital Region Innovation Hub
restrictions.description: Rensselaer County (Troy) is home to Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute and anchors the eastern Capital Region's tech valley initiative. National Grid service territory, New York's Excelsior Jobs Program incentives, and proximity to the Capital Region fiber ring position it as a secondary data center market. SUNY Poly and Albany NanoTech complex drive demand for research compute infrastructure.

sources.label: New York State Excelsior Jobs Program

restrictions.name: Hunterdon County
restrictions.title: Central NJ Exurb — Data Center Candidate on Route 78 Corridor
restrictions.description: Hunterdon County (Flemington, Clinton) sits along the I-78 corridor in central New Jersey. JCP&L/FirstEnergy service territory, proximity to the Somerset County data center cluster, and lower industrial land costs than Union or Somerset counties make select Hunterdon locations candidates for large-footprint data center campuses. No county-level data center ordinances.

restrictions.name: Lawrence County
restrictions.title: New Castle — Ohio River Industrial Legacy Power Infrastructure
restrictions.description: Lawrence County (New Castle) sits northwest of Pittsburgh on the Ohio River corridor and retains substantial industrial-grade power infrastructure from its steel legacy. FirstEnergy/Illuminating Company service, PIDA grant eligibility, and Pennsylvania's absence of a specific data center tax program make it suitable for power-intensive deployments benefiting from legacy infrastructure.

sources.label: Pennsylvania Department of Community and Economic Development

restrictions.name: Butler County
restrictions.title: Pittsburgh North Suburb — Growing Industrial and Tech Zone
restrictions.description: Butler County (Butler, Cranberry Township) is the fastest-growing county in western Pennsylvania and anchors Pittsburgh's northern tech and industrial corridor. West Penn Power/FirstEnergy service, the county's attractiveness for corporate relocations along PA 228 and I-79, and proximity to Pittsburgh International Airport's fiber infrastructure make it a data center consideration.

restrictions.name: Washington County
restrictions.title: Pittsburgh South — Marcellus Shale and Industrial Power Access
restrictions.description: Washington County (Washington, Canonsburg) lies south of Pittsburgh and has benefited from Marcellus Shale natural gas development, providing competitive industrial power. FirstEnergy/West Penn Power service, I-79 and I-70 corridor logistics, and the county's tradition of heavy industry make it a candidate for power-intensive data center deployments.

restrictions.name: Indiana County
restrictions.title: Indiana — IUP and Rural Appalachian Fiber Corridor
restrictions.description: Indiana County (Indiana) hosts Indiana University of Pennsylvania and sits on a key fiber route across the Laurel Highlands. Penelec/FirstEnergy service and the county's position along US 422 connecting Pittsburgh and Philadelphia provide some data center relevance, primarily for educational computing and distributed infrastructure.

sources.label: Indiana County Department of Planning and Development

restrictions.name: Calvert County
restrictions.title: Southern Maryland — DC-Adjacent Emerging Market
restrictions.description: Calvert County (Prince Frederick) sits along the western shore of the Chesapeake Bay south of Prince George's County. Growing as a DC-adjacent residential and light-industrial area, it benefits from Pepco/Exelon service reliability and proximity to the National Capital Region fiber ring. The county's Southern Maryland Technology Corridor initiative supports tech investment.

sources.label: Calvert County Department of Economic Development

restrictions.name: Davidson County
restrictions.title: NC Triad — Lexington Industrial Hub and Duke Energy Access
restrictions.description: Davidson County (Lexington, Thomasville) occupies the center of the North Carolina Triad region between Greensboro and Winston-Salem. Duke Energy Carolinas service with competitive industrial rates, the county's industrial heritage (furniture manufacturing), Tier 2 county status for NC economic development incentives, and I-85 corridor access make it attractive for data center investment.

sources.label: Duke Energy — Data Center Solutions NC

restrictions.name: Granville County
restrictions.title: Raleigh North — Research Triangle Overflow and Duke Energy
restrictions.description: Granville County (Oxford) lies north of Wake County and directly captures Research Triangle overflow demand as land and power costs in the Triangle core have escalated. Duke Energy Progress service, Tier 2 NC county incentives, and the US 158/I-85 corridor give it strong site-selection characteristics for data center campuses seeking rural land at lower cost.

sources.label: NC Department of Commerce — Site Selection

restrictions.name: Rockingham County
restrictions.title: NC Triad North — Eden Industrial Corridor and Duke Energy
restrictions.description: Rockingham County (Reidsville, Eden) anchors the northern Triad region and is a Tier 1 distressed county in NC's economic development program, qualifying for maximum state incentive levels. Duke Energy Carolinas service, large tracts of former industrial land (textiles), and US 220/US 29 corridor access make it a candidate for large-footprint data center campuses where incentives can be maximized.

restrictions.name: Lancaster County
restrictions.title: Charlotte Metro South — Duke Energy and SC Incentives
restrictions.description: Lancaster County (Lancaster) anchors the southern Charlotte metro in South Carolina and benefits from a powerful combination of incentives: SC's no inventory tax, Fee in Lieu of Taxes (FILOT) agreements allowing 6% assessment ratio instead of 10.5%, Duke Energy Carolinas service, and a Tier IV (highest) SC Coordinating Council designation qualifying for maximum state incentives.

restrictions.name: St. Johns County
restrictions.title: Jacksonville South Suburb — Ponte Vedra and St. Augustine Growth
restrictions.description: St. Johns County (St. Augustine, Ponte Vedra Beach) is the fastest-growing county in Florida by percentage and the wealthiest in the Northeast Florida region. Florida Power & Light service, rapidly expanding fiber infrastructure along I-95, and the county's strong economic development posture make it an emerging candidate for edge and enterprise data center deployments serving the Jacksonville MSA.

restrictions.name: Sevier County
restrictions.title: Smokies Corridor — TVA Power and Oak Ridge Tech Adjacency
restrictions.description: Sevier County (Sevierville, Gatlinburg, Pigeon Forge) sits adjacent to Anderson County's Oak Ridge National Laboratory complex in the TVA service territory. TVA's competitive industrial power rates, the county's growing commercial base, and proximity to ORNL's leadership computing facilities create some data center interest. Tennessee's no-income-tax advantage and state incentive programs apply.

restrictions.name: Jefferson County
restrictions.title: St. Louis South — I-55 Corridor Growth
restrictions.description: Jefferson County (Festus, Arnold) anchors the southern St. Louis MSA along I-55. Ameren Missouri service, lower industrial land costs relative to St. Louis County, and a growing tech industrial base driven by the broader metro's data center expansion make it a candidate for colocation and edge deployments. The county's Meramec River corridor provides additional site diversity.

restrictions.name: Callaway County
restrictions.title: Columbia MO Area — University Research Infrastructure
restrictions.description: Callaway County (Fulton) lies east of Columbia in the Union Electric/Ameren Missouri service area and adjacent to Callaway Nuclear Plant — one of Missouri's primary power generation assets. The county's proximity to University of Missouri and related research computing infrastructure, combined with competitive power access, creates data center relevance.

restrictions.name: Porter County
restrictions.title: Chicago Metro East — I-90 Corridor and NIPSCO Service
restrictions.description: Porter County (Valparaiso, Portage) anchors Indiana's northwestern corner along the I-90/I-80 Chicago-Detroit corridor. Northern Indiana Public Service Company (NIPSCO) provides competitive industrial rates with significant renewable transition underway. The county's proximity to Chicago metro fiber routes, lower Indiana industrial property tax rates, and I-94 access make it relevant for data center site selection as an alternative to more expensive Cook County IL sites.

restrictions.name: Iberville Parish
restrictions.title: Plaquemine — Petrochemical Power Infrastructure and ITEP
restrictions.description: Iberville Parish (Plaquemine) hosts a dense petrochemical industrial complex along the Mississippi River with abundant industrial-grade power infrastructure. Louisiana's Industrial Tax Exemption Program (ITEP) offers 100% property tax abatement for qualifying manufacturing facilities including data centers. Entergy Louisiana service with interruptible industrial rates and fiber along the River Road corridor.

restrictions.name: West Baton Rouge Parish
restrictions.title: Port Allen — I-10 Corridor and Mississippi River Power Hub
restrictions.description: West Baton Rouge Parish (Port Allen) sits directly across the Mississippi River from Baton Rouge, offering access to the full Baton Rouge MSA infrastructure at lower land costs. Entergy Louisiana service, Louisiana ITEP 100% property tax abatement, I-10 fiber corridor, and cross-river bridge access to Baton Rouge's tech and university economy make it a data center candidate.

sources.label: West Baton Rouge Parish — Business Development

restrictions.name: Richmond County
restrictions.title: Augusta — US Army Cyber Command and ARCYBER Hub
restrictions.description: Richmond County (Augusta) hosts Fort Eisenhower (formerly Fort Gordon), home to US Army Cyber Command (ARCYBER), the National Security Agency's Georgia node, and the Cyber Center of Excellence. The county is one of the most significant US cyber and defense data infrastructure locations in the Southeast. Augusta University supports a growing cyber and AI research ecosystem. Georgia's O.C.G.A. § 48-8-3.2 data center equipment sales tax exemption applies. No local restrictions.

sources.label: US Army Cyber Command — Fort Eisenhower

restrictions.name: Chatham County
restrictions.title: Savannah — Port Authority and Regional Logistics Data Hub
restrictions.description: Chatham County (Savannah) anchors Georgia's largest port complex. The Port of Savannah's massive Amazon and logistics footprint drives demand for edge and regional data center infrastructure. Georgia Power service territory, the state's data center tax exemption, and a growing tech workforce in the Historic Savannah/Pooler corridor position the county as an emerging secondary market.

restrictions.name: Tuscaloosa County
restrictions.title: Tuscaloosa — University of Alabama and Mercedes-Benz Tech Corridor
restrictions.description: Tuscaloosa County hosts the University of Alabama and the Mercedes-Benz US manufacturing complex, driving significant enterprise IT and edge data center demand. Alabama Power competitive industrial rates, Alabama's data center incentives under the Alabama Jobs Act, and the county's I-20/59 corridor connectivity make it a growing secondary market.

restrictions.name: Crittenden County
restrictions.title: West Memphis — Memphis Metro River Crossing Data Corridor
restrictions.description: Crittenden County (West Memphis) sits at the Arkansas end of the Memphis, TN MSA across the Mississippi River. Lower industrial land costs than Shelby County TN, Arkansas's favorable tax environment (no inventory tax, data center sales tax exemption Act 182 of 2019), and critical fiber crossings at the I-40 and I-55 bridges make it an established data center alternative to the more expensive Tennessee side.

sources.label: Arkansas Economic Development Commission — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Scott County
restrictions.title: Georgetown — Toyota and Amazon Logistics Infrastructure Hub
restrictions.description: Scott County (Georgetown) hosts Toyota Motor Manufacturing Kentucky, the largest Toyota plant in North America, and has attracted substantial Amazon logistics and delivery infrastructure. Kentucky Utilities service, Kentucky's data center incentives under KRS 139.497 (sales tax exemption on equipment and power), and the I-75 corridor make it a growing data center market as automotive tech drives IT demand.

restrictions.name: Jessamine County
restrictions.title: Nicholasville — Lexington Southern Suburb and KU/Data Growth
restrictions.description: Jessamine County (Nicholasville) anchors the southern Lexington MSA along US 27 and the KY 9 Mountain Parkway corridor. Kentucky Utilities service, Kentucky's data center tax incentive program, and the county's position in the Lexington labor and fiber market at lower industrial land costs make it an emerging consideration for enterprise and edge data center deployments.

restrictions.name: Warren County
restrictions.title: Bowling Green — EV Manufacturing, Amazon, and Transpark Growth
restrictions.description: Warren County (Bowling Green) is one of the fastest-growing counties in Kentucky, anchored by the GM EV manufacturing plant (Ultium Cells), Corvette assembly, and a major Amazon fulfillment complex. Kentucky Utilities service, Kentucky's data center equipment and power sales tax exemption, and the Bowling Green-Warren County Transpark industrial zone make it a priority target for edge data center development serving advanced manufacturing IT demand.

sources.label: Bowling Green Area Chamber of Commerce

restrictions.name: Lamar County
restrictions.title: Hattiesburg Metro — Pine Belt Regional Hub
restrictions.description: Lamar County (Purvis, Lumberton) anchors the western Hattiesburg MSA and is one of Mississippi's fastest-growing counties. Entergy Mississippi service with competitive industrial rates, Mississippi's data center sales tax exemption (Miss. Code § 27-65-101), and the county's proximity to I-59 and the University of Southern Mississippi create some data center relevance for the Pine Belt region.

restrictions.name: Washington County
restrictions.title: Blair — Omaha North Suburb and NPPD Territory
restrictions.description: Washington County (Blair, Fort Calhoun) lies north of Douglas County (Omaha) in the Nebraska Public Power District service territory. The county's proximity to the Omaha metro data center cluster, NPPD competitive industrial rates with significant nuclear generation (Cooper Nuclear Station), and Nebraska's data center sales tax exemption (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 77-2704.65) make it an extension of the Omaha market.

sources.label: Nebraska Department of Economic Development — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Pottawatomie County
restrictions.title: Manhattan Area — Kansas State University Research Computing
restrictions.description: Pottawatomie County anchors the western side of the Manhattan MSA (Riley County is the core). Kansas State University's AI and research computing expansion, Westar/Evergy service with competitive wind-heavy renewable rates, and the Manhattan area's growing data science and agricultural AI sector create data center relevance in this university corridor.

sources.label: Kansas Department of Commerce — Data Centers

restrictions.name: Rogers County
restrictions.title: Claremore — Tulsa Eastern Suburb and PSO Service Territory
restrictions.description: Rogers County (Claremore) anchors the eastern Tulsa MSA along I-44. Public Service Company of Oklahoma (PSO/AEP) service with competitive Oklahoma industrial rates, Oklahoma's data center sales tax exemption (68 O.S. § 1355.4), and lower industrial land costs than Tulsa County make it a consideration for data center operators expanding in the Tulsa market.

restrictions.name: Portage County
restrictions.title: Kent-Ravenna — Kent State and Northeast Ohio Industrial
restrictions.description: Portage County (Kent, Ravenna) hosts Kent State University and anchors a northeast Ohio industrial corridor between Cleveland and Akron. FirstEnergy/Ohio Edison service territory, Ohio's data center equipment exemption from sales tax (R.C. § 5739.02(B)), and the county's industrial heritage with competitive power access make it relevant for edge and research computing deployments.

restrictions.name: Blount County
restrictions.title: Alcoa and Maryville — TVA Industrial Power and ORNL Adjacency
restrictions.description: Blount County (Maryville, Alcoa) anchors the southern Knoxville MSA and hosts a major Alcoa smelter, providing proof of large-scale industrial power access. TVA service through Knoxville Utilities Board, proximity to Oak Ridge National Laboratory's computational resources, Tennessee's no-income-tax advantage, and a growing technology corridor along US 129 make it a data center candidate.

restrictions.name: Champaign County
restrictions.title: Champaign-Urbana — UIUC National Center for Supercomputing
restrictions.description: Champaign County hosts the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, home to the National Center for Supercomputing Applications (NCSA) and one of the most significant research computing concentrations in the US. Ameren Illinois service, Illinois' High Impact Business data center program, and the county's concentration of AI/ML startups and corporate R&D operations create substantial data center demand.

sources.label: National Center for Supercomputing Applications — UIUC
Illinois Department of Commerce — High Impact Business

restrictions.name: Allen County
restrictions.title: Fort Wayne — Northeast Indiana Regional Hub and I&M Service
restrictions.description: Allen County (Fort Wayne) is the second-largest city in Indiana and the regional hub for northeast Indiana. Indiana & Michigan Power (AEP) service with competitive industrial rates, Indiana's data center tax incentive program (IC 6-1.1-10-44), I-69 and I-469 connectivity, and the city's growing diversified industrial base position it as a data center destination for northern Indiana deployments.

sources.label: Greater Fort Wayne Chamber of Commerce

restrictions.name: Burlington County
restrictions.title: Burlington — Major NJ Suburb and PSE&G Corporate Corridor
restrictions.description: Burlington County (Mount Holly, Moorestown, Evesham) is one of the largest and most industrially active counties in New Jersey along I-295. PSE&G service territory with high reliability, proximity to Philadelphia metro fiber rings, New Jersey's Economic Redevelopment and Growth (ERG) Grant Program, and lower land costs than Camden or Middlesex counties make it a growing data center location.

restrictions.name: Ocean County
restrictions.title: Toms River — NJ Shore Corridor and JCP&L Service Territory
restrictions.description: Ocean County (Toms River, Lakewood) anchors the northern New Jersey Shore corridor south of Monmouth County. JCP&L (FirstEnergy) service, access to I-195/Garden State Parkway fiber routes, and the county's growing tech-adjacent residential base drive enterprise data center demand. Industrial zoning in the Route 9 corridor provides site availability for medium-scale deployments.

restrictions.name: Broome County
restrictions.title: Binghamton — IBM Legacy and Binghamton University AI Research
restrictions.description: Broome County (Binghamton) has deep roots in computing through IBM's founding era and today hosts Binghamton University, a SUNY campus with growing AI and smart energy research programs. NYSEG (Avangrid) service, New York's Excelsior Jobs Program data center credits, and the county's legacy technology infrastructure position it as a data center candidate for distributed compute and research workloads.

sources.label: New York State Excelsior Jobs Program

restrictions.name: Niagara County
restrictions.title: Niagara Falls — Cheap Hydroelectric Power and Industrial Transition
restrictions.description: Niagara County (Niagara Falls, Lockport) hosts the Niagara Power Project, generating approximately 2,700 MW from the Niagara River. The Power Authority of New York (NYPA) has targeted this low-cost, renewable power specifically for data center attraction, offering Power Allocation to Businesses (PACKAGED POWER) and Economic Development Power programs. Former aluminum smelter sites provide high-power industrial infrastructure for conversion.

sources.label: New York Power Authority — Niagara Power Project
Niagara County Center for Economic Development

restrictions.name: Bristol County
restrictions.title: New Bedford-Taunton — Southeastern MA and Eversource Service
restrictions.description: Bristol County (New Bedford, Taunton, Fall River, Attleboro) anchors southeastern Massachusetts between Boston and Providence. Eversource Energy service with New England grid reliability, the Route 44 and I-495 tech corridor, and the county's industrial heritage transitioning to tech-adjacent uses create data center consideration. Massachusetts Clean Energy Center programs support data center renewable commitments.

restrictions.name: Winnebago County
restrictions.title: Oshkosh — Fox Valley Paper Industry Transition and WPS Service
restrictions.description: Winnebago County (Oshkosh, Neenah, Menasha) anchors the Fox Valley and was historically a paper and manufacturing center. Wisconsin Public Service (WPS) competitive industrial rates, the county's industrial power infrastructure from its paper mill era, and growing tech diversification following UW-Oshkosh's manufacturing engineering programs make it relevant for edge and industrial data center deployments.

sources.label: Oshkosh Chamber of Commerce and Economic Development

restrictions.name: Lucas County
restrictions.title: Toledo — 4th-Largest Ohio City and AEP/Toledo Edison Service
restrictions.description: Lucas County (Toledo) is Ohio's fourth-largest urban center and a significant industrial base on Lake Erie. Toledo Edison (FirstEnergy) service with Ohio's data center sales tax exemption (R.C. § 5739.02(B)), I-75/I-80/I-90 corridor fiber access, and the county's industrial land availability at lower costs than Columbus or Cleveland make it a secondary data center market for northern Ohio.

restrictions.name: Butler County
restrictions.title: Hamilton — Cincinnati Northern Suburb and Duke Energy Ohio Territory
restrictions.description: Butler County (Hamilton, Middletown, Oxford) anchors the Cincinnati northern suburb corridor. Duke Energy Ohio and Cincinnati Bell service, Ohio's data center equipment sales tax exemption, I-75 connectivity, and Miami University in Oxford create a growing data center and enterprise IT presence in the northern Cincinnati MSA.

restrictions.name: Stark County
restrictions.title: Canton-Massillon — Akron-Canton Industrial Corridor and AEP Territory
restrictions.description: Stark County (Canton, Massillon, Alliance) anchors the Akron-Canton industrial corridor between Cleveland and Columbus. AEP Ohio service territory with competitive industrial rates, Ohio's data center sales tax exemption, available industrial land from manufacturing transitions, and the county's central Ohio position on the I-77 corridor make it a viable secondary data center market.

restrictions.name: Dauphin County
restrictions.title: Harrisburg — Pennsylvania State Capital IT Hub and PPL Territory
restrictions.description: Dauphin County (Harrisburg) is Pennsylvania's state capital and a significant government IT center. PPL Electric Utilities service, concentration of state government and federal agency data infrastructure, I-78/I-81/I-83 interchange connectivity, and Pennsylvania's keystone opportunity zone program make it a natural data center location for government cloud and enterprise deployments.

sources.label: Pennsylvania Department of Community and Economic Development

restrictions.name: Cumberland County
restrictions.title: Camp Hill-Carlisle — Harrisburg West Suburb and Lower-Cost PA Hub
restrictions.description: Cumberland County (Camp Hill, Carlisle, Mechanicsburg) anchors the western Harrisburg metro and hosts one of Pennsylvania's most active industrial park corridors along I-81. PPL Electric Utilities service, lower land costs than Dauphin County while sharing the same metro fiber and workforce, and the PA Turnpike I-76 interchange at Carlisle make it a preferred data center location for Harrisburg-area deployments.

sources.label: Pennsylvania Department of Community and Economic Development

restrictions.name: Lee County
restrictions.title: Fort Myers-Cape Coral — SW Florida's Fastest-Growing Metro
restrictions.description: Lee County (Fort Myers, Cape Coral) is one of the fastest-growing metros in the United States, anchored by healthcare (Lee Health), retirement and financial services, and rapidly expanding tech-adjacent industries. Florida Power & Light service, Florida's no-corporate-income-tax advantage for data center equipment, and the county's exploding commercial real estate supply make it an emerging data center market.

restrictions.name: Osceola County
restrictions.title: Kissimmee-St. Cloud — Orlando South Corridor and Theme Park Tech
restrictions.description: Osceola County (Kissimmee, St. Cloud, Celebration) anchors the southern Orlando MSA and hosts a massive hospitality and theme park industry infrastructure. Duke Energy Florida and FPL service across the county, the US 192 and Florida Turnpike data center corridor, and Florida's data center equipment exemption from sales tax (F.S. § 212.08(5)(f)) make it a relevant market for hospitality-sector IT and edge computing.

restrictions.name: St. Lucie County
restrictions.title: Port St. Lucie — Treasure Coast Growth Market and FPL Territory
restrictions.description: St. Lucie County (Port St. Lucie, Fort Pierce) is one of Florida's fastest-growing counties along the Treasure Coast between West Palm Beach and Melbourne. Florida Power & Light service with high reliability, Florida's data center equipment sales tax exemption, I-95 and US 1 fiber routes, and lower commercial land costs than the Palm Beach County market make it an emerging consideration for edge and secondary data center deployment.

restrictions.name: Sarasota County
restrictions.title: Sarasota — Gulf Coast Financial Services and FPL Service Territory
restrictions.description: Sarasota County (Sarasota, Venice, North Port) hosts a substantial financial services, healthcare, and professional services sector drawing significant enterprise IT demand. Florida Power & Light service, Florida's data center sales tax exemption, the US 41 corridor industrial sites, and the county's high-income workforce demographic supporting enterprise tech make it a secondary data center market on Florida's Gulf Coast.

sources.label: Economic Development Corporation of Sarasota County

restrictions.name: Volusia County
restrictions.title: Daytona Beach-Deltona — Central Florida Spine and Duke Energy Service
restrictions.description: Volusia County (Daytona Beach, Deltona, Deland) anchors the northeastern Orlando-Daytona corridor along I-4. Duke Energy Florida service, Embry-Riddle Aeronautical University's growing simulation and aerospace IT demand, Florida's data center equipment sales tax exemption, and the county's industrial park availability along I-95 and I-4 make it relevant for Central Florida data center expansion.

restrictions.name: Brown County
restrictions.title: Green Bay — Northeast Wisconsin Hub and WPS/Integrys Service
restrictions.description: Brown County (Green Bay) is Wisconsin's third-largest city and the regional center for northeast Wisconsin. Wisconsin Public Service (WPS/Integrys) competitive industrial rates, growing healthcare IT (Bellin and HSHS systems), the Green Bay Titletown Tech corridor, and Wisconsin's no-data-center-specific-restriction environment make it a regional data center market for northeast Wisconsin enterprise deployments.

sources.label: Green Bay Area Chamber of Commerce — EDC

restrictions.name: La Crosse County
restrictions.title: La Crosse — Upper Mississippi Hub and Dairyland Power Cooperative
restrictions.description: La Crosse County anchors the Upper Mississippi River corridor between Minneapolis and Madison. Dairyland Power Cooperative service with competitive wholesale rates and growing renewable mix, University of Wisconsin-La Crosse creating a research computing base, and the county's strategic position at the junction of I-90 and US 53 with Mississippi River fiber crossings make it a secondary Wisconsin data center market.

restrictions.name: Stearns County
restrictions.title: St. Cloud — Microsoft Data Center Hub and Xcel Energy Renewable
restrictions.description: Stearns County (St. Cloud) is home to one of Microsoft's major Azure data center campuses, choosing the area for its Xcel Energy access, cold-climate cooling advantages, fiber connectivity, and competitive power rates. Xcel Energy's heavy wind and nuclear portfolio in the region, Minnesota's Enterprise Zone program, and the county's established data center workforce make it one of the most significant data center markets in the Upper Midwest.

sources.label: Minnesota Department of Employment and Economic Development

restrictions.name: St. Louis County
restrictions.title: Duluth — Minnesota Power Low-Cost Hydro and Iron Range Digital
restrictions.description: St. Louis County (Duluth, Hibbing, Virginia MN) is Minnesota's largest county by area and hosts significant low-cost hydro and renewable electricity via Minnesota Power (ALLETE). The Iron Range's industrial power infrastructure, Great Lakes fiber access at the Duluth harbor, University of Minnesota Duluth computing programs, and some of the lowest electricity rates in the state make it a niche data center market for power-intensive workloads.

restrictions.name: Blue Earth County
restrictions.title: Mankato — Southern Minnesota Education and Industrial Hub
restrictions.description: Blue Earth County (Mankato, North Mankato) anchors southern Minnesota between the Twin Cities and Sioux Falls. Minnesota State University-Mankato's IT and computer science programs, Southern Minnesota Energy Cooperative service, I-90/US 169 connectivity, and the county's growing medical device and manufacturing sector create enterprise IT and edge computing demand.

sources.label: Minnesota Department of Employment and Economic Development

restrictions.name: Baldwin County
restrictions.title: Daphne-Fairhope — Gulf Coast Growth and PowerSouth Territory
restrictions.description: Baldwin County (Daphne, Fairhope, Foley, Orange Beach) is Alabama's fastest-growing county along the Gulf Coast. PowerSouth Energy Cooperative service, Alabama's data center incentives under the Alabama Jobs Act, I-10 corridor connectivity linking Mobile to Pensacola, and the county's rapid residential and commercial growth driving enterprise IT demand make it an emerging secondary data center consideration in the Gulf Coast market.

restrictions.name: Monroe County
restrictions.title: Monroe — SE Michigan Industrial and DTE Energy Territory
restrictions.description: Monroe County (Monroe, Bedford) sits at the southeastern tip of Michigan between Detroit and Toledo on Lake Erie. DTE Energy service with large-scale industrial power capacity from legacy Monroe Power Plant infrastructure transitioning to renewables, I-75 corridor connectivity, and lower industrial land costs than Wayne County make it a candidate for large-scale data center deployments in the Detroit exurb.

restrictions.name: Muskegon County
restrictions.title: Muskegon — Lake Michigan Port and Consumers Energy Offshore Wind Hub
restrictions.description: Muskegon County (Muskegon, Muskegon Heights) sits on Lake Michigan's east shore and is positioned as a key hub for Consumers Energy's Lake Michigan offshore wind development. The county's industrial brownfield sites from paper and manufacturing transitions, Consumers Energy service with growing renewable capacity, deepwater Lake Michigan access for cooling, and Michigan's data center infrastructure program make it a niche but growing data center consideration.

restrictions.name: Madison County
restrictions.title: Jackson — Western Tennessee Regional Hub and TVA Industrial Power
restrictions.description: Madison County (Jackson) is the primary commercial and healthcare hub for western Tennessee between Memphis and Nashville. TVA industrial power service through Jackson Energy Authority (JEA), Tennessee's no-income-tax advantage, US 45 and I-40 connectivity, and the county's regional hospital systems and university campus driving enterprise IT demand make it a data center consideration for western Tennessee deployments.

restrictions.name: Oldham County
restrictions.title: La Grange — Louisville NE Suburb and LG&E Service Territory
restrictions.description: Oldham County (La Grange, Crestwood) is Louisville's fastest-growing northeastern suburb. Louisville Gas & Electric (LG&E/PPL) service, Kentucky's data center equipment sales tax exemption (KRS 139.497), I-71 corridor connectivity, and the county's lower industrial land costs compared to Jefferson County (Louisville) make it an attractive location for data center operators seeking Louisville market access at lower cost.

restrictions.name: Peoria County
restrictions.title: Peoria — Caterpillar HQ, OSF Healthcare, and Ameren Illinois Service
restrictions.description: Peoria County is Illinois' third-largest metro and home to Caterpillar's global headquarters, OSF HealthCare, and Methodist Health System, all driving major enterprise IT demand. Ameren Illinois service with the Illinois High Impact Business data center incentive, I-74/I-474 corridor connectivity, and the county's industrial heritage with available large-site land make it a secondary data center market for central Illinois.

sources.label: Tri-County Regional Planning Commission Economic Development
Illinois Department of Commerce — High Impact Business

restrictions.name: Rock Island County
restrictions.title: Quad Cities (IL Side) — John Deere HQ and MidAmerican Energy Territory
restrictions.description: Rock Island County (Rock Island, Moline) anchors the Illinois side of the Quad Cities metro, home to John Deere's world headquarters and a significant manufacturing and ag-tech corridor. MidAmerican Energy (Berkshire Hathaway) service with a heavy wind renewable portfolio and competitive industrial rates, Illinois' High Impact Business program, and I-74/I-80 fiber accessibility make the Quad Cities a growing data center market.
